coredata.dc:title: Tetraphenylethylene-connected monoquaternized 4-(4-pyridyl)pyridinium: A colorimetric probe for detecting trace amounts of water in organic solvents
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Jose, Karolin P.
Priyadharsan, R. Rameshbabu
Chidambaranathan, Kamalanathan
Karthikeyan, Subramanian
Anthony, Savarimuthu Philip
Madhu, Vedichi
coredata.dc:description: Tetraphenylethylene (TPE) and triphenylamine (TPA) electron donor attached mono-quaternized 4-(4-pyridyl)pyridinium derivatives, (1-(2-oxo-2-(4-(1,2,2-triphenylvinyl)phenyl)ethyl)-[4,4′-bipyridin]-1-ium bromide (TPE-MQ) and 1-(2-(4-(diphenylamino)phenyl)-2-oxoethyl)-[4,4′-bipyridin]-1-ium bromide (TPA-MQ) were synthesized and investigated the role of TPE/TPA in tuning their optical properties. Both TPE-MQ and TPA-MQ showed strong intramolecular charge transfer (ICT), which was confirmed through HOMO/LUMO calculations and solvent polarity dependent absorption studies. Interestingly, the acidic methylene of TPE-MQ was found to be highly sensitive to water compared to TPA-MQ. Even trace amounts of water in organic solvents acted as nucleophiles, leading to keto-enol formation and intense color due to enhanced π-conjugation. In contrast, TPA-MQ did not display any color or absorption changes in the presence of water. The water-induced structural conversion from keto to enol form was confirmed using 1H NMR analysis. Further, TPE-MQ colorimetric probe displayed clear color changes in presence of trace amounts of water in polar protic, aprotic, and non-polar solvents. Thus, the present study developed a simple naked-dye detecting colorimetric probe for sensing trace amount of water in organic solvents including commercial solvents.

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authlast grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst orcid primabst ref

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2025 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: UGC-DAE Consortium for Scientific Research in Indore
Karunya Institute of Technology and Sciences
DAE Consortium for Scientific Research in Indore

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Financial support from the Science and Engineering Research Board ( SERB ), EMR/2016/006106, New Delhi, India, is acknowledged with gratitude. VM also acknowledges the UGC- DAE Consortium for Scientific Research in Indore for their financial support ( CRS /2021-22/04/641). Additionally, KPJ thanks the Karunya Institute of Technology and Sciences for providing research associate support.

2-Bromoacetophenone functionalized tetraphenylethylene (TPE) and triphenylamine (TPA) derivatives, referred to as BA-TPE and BA-TPA were synthesized using a previously reported procedure from our research group [54]. The mono-quaternized N-phenacyl-4-(4-pyridyl)pyridinium bromide derivatives, TPE-MQ and TPA-MQ, were synthesized by reacting 4,4\u2032-bipyridine with either BA-TPE or BA-TPA in anhydrous acetonitrile at 90 \u00B0C for 3 h. This reaction was also performed in anhydrous acetone at room temperature, as shown in Scheme 2. When acetonitrile was used as the solvent, the reaction primarily produced a mono-quaternary N-phenacyl-4-(4-pyridyl)pyridinium salt as the major product. Additionally, a minor product, the bi-quaternary 4,4\u2032-bipyridinium salt, was also formed. However, both products are insoluble in anhydrous acetonitrile, making their separation challenging. In contrast, when the reaction was conducted in anhydrous acetone at room temperature, it selectively yielded the mono-quaternary N-phenacyl-4-(4-pyridyl)pyridinium salt without producing the bi-quaternary 4,4\u2032-bipyridinium salt, which also remains insoluble in anhydrous acetone [55]. The synthesized compounds, TPE-MQ and TPA-MQ, were characterized using FT-IR, 1H and 13C NMR, and mass spectral analysis. The FT-IR analysis of TPE-MQ and TPA-MQ shows that the absorption bands at 1691 cm\u22121 and 1674 cm\u22121 correspond to the fundamental vibrations of the \u03BD(C[dbnd]O) functional group. The lower vibrational frequency indicates the presence of N-phenacyl pyridinium ketones, as well as a carbonyl functional group that is conjugated with a benzene ring. Both compounds exhibit two distinct vibrational frequency bands. The first band, observed in the range of 1635\u20131639 cm\u22121, corresponds to the free C[dbnd]N stretch (\u03BD(C[dbnd]N)). The second band occurs in the range of 1587\u20131599 cm\u22121 and is associated with the N-alkylated C[dbnd]N+ stretch (\u03BD(C[dbnd]N+)). Notably, the N-alkylated \u03BD(C[dbnd]N+) group demonstrates a lower vibrational frequency than the free \u03BD(C[dbnd]N) group. This observation suggests a decreased double bond character for the \u03BD(C[dbnd]N+) group. Therefore, the FT-IR results confirm the presence of both C[dbnd]N and C[dbnd]N+ groups, thereby supporting the formation of monoquaternized 4-(4-pyridyl)pyridinium units (Fig. S2 and S4). 1H NMR analysis showed a singlet signal at 6.48 ppm for TPE-MQ and 6.39 ppm for TPA-MQ, which correspond to the CH2 protons of the 2-oxoethyl (\u2013CH2\u2013C[dbnd]O) group. The chemical shifts of aromatic protons adjacent to the pyridinium nitrogen were observed in the range between 9.25 and 9.23 ppm for TPE-MQ and 9.11 and 9.13 ppm for TPA-MQ. The downfield chemical shift of the pyridinium protons suggests that one of the nitrogens in the 4,4\u2032-bipyridine unit has become electron deficient due to N-alkylation. TPE-MQ and TPA-MQ exhibited three doublet signals between \u03B4 8.90\u20137.27 ppm and 8.91\u20138.07 ppm, which are attributed to the remaining aromatic protons of the 4,4\u2032-bipyridine unit. The upfield chemical shift indicates that one pyridine moiety in the 4,4\u2032-bipyridine unit remains free from N-alkylation. Peaks for the aromatic protons of TPE and TPA were found in the range of 7.95\u20136.95 ppm. The representative 1H NMR spectra of TPE-MQ and TPA-MQ are shown in Fig. S5 and S7. In the 13C NMR spectra of TPE-MQ and TPA-MQ showed aromatic carbon peaks between 153.33 and 118.51 ppm and \u2013CH2- carbon of 2-oxoethyl group at 66.79 and 65.79 ppm (Fig. S6 and S8).Financial support from the Science and Engineering Research Board (SERB), EMR/2016/006106, New Delhi, India, is acknowledged with gratitude. VM also acknowledges the UGC-DAE Consortium for Scientific Research in Indore for their financial support (CRS/2021-22/04/641). Additionally, KPJ thanks the Karunya Institute of Technology and Sciences for providing research associate support.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Photographic images demonstrating the solvatochromism behavior of TPE-MQ in various solvents (1.0 × 10−5 M) and (b) absorbance spectra of TPE-MQ in different solvents (1.0 × 10−5 M).
1H NMR spectra of TPE-MQ in dry DMSO‑d 6 (a) and water spiked DMSO‑d 6 (b).
(a) The effect of water addition on TPE-MQ (10−5 M) absorption in ethanol. (b) The limit of water detection in ethanol.
The changes in the absorption of TPE-MQ (10−5 M) in CH3CN with the addition of water.
The changes in the absorption characteristics of TPE-MQ (10−5 M) in (a) DMF and (b) DMSO upon the addition of water.

The optimized ground-state distributions of HOMO and LUMO, along with their corresponding energy values and the optical band gap, for (a) TPE-MQ, (b) TPA-MQ, (c) TPE-MQ-E, and (d) TPA-MQ-E were obtained through density functional theory (DFT) calculations utilizing the B3PW91/6-31+G(d,p) model.
Structure of TPE-MQ and TPA-MQ colorimetric sensors used in this study.
The synthetic route for preparing TPE-MQ and TPA-MQ.
Proposed mechanism for water induced enol (TPE-MQ-E/TPA-MQ-E) formation from the keto compounds TPE-MQ and TPA-MQ.

head.ce:title: Tetraphenylethylene-connected monoquaternized 4-(4-pyridyl)pyridinium: A colorimetric probe for detecting trace amounts of water in organic solvents

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Multifunctional Materials and Catalysis Laboratory, Division of Physical Sciences, Karunya Institute of Technology and Sciences, Coimbatore-641 114, Tamil Nadu, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Karunya Institute of Technology and Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Multifunctional Materials and Catalysis Laboratory, Division of Physical Sciences, Karunya Institute of Technology and Sciences, Coimbatore-641 114, Tamil Nadu, India. E-mail

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical & Biotechnology, SASTRA Deemed University, Thanjavur- 613401, Tamil Nadu, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemical & Biotechnology, SASTRA Deemed University, Thanjavur- 613401, Tamil Nadu, India. E-mail:

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: PG and Research Department of Chemistry, KhadirMohideen College (affiliated to Bharathidasan University), Adirampattinam, Tamil Nadu, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: PG and Research Department of Chemistry

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: PG and Research Department of Chemistry, KhadirMohideen College (affiliated to Bharathidasan University), Adirampattinam, Tamil Nadu, India.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Tetraphenylethylene (TPE) and triphenylamine (TPA) electron donor attached mono-quaternized 4-(4-pyridyl)pyridinium derivatives, (1-(2-oxo-2-(4-(1,2,2-triphenylvinyl)phenyl)ethyl)-[4,4′-bipyridin]-1-ium bromide (TPE-MQ) and 1-(2-(4-(diphenylamino)phenyl)-2-oxoethyl)-[4,4′-bipyridin]-1-ium bromide (TPA-MQ) were synthesized and investigated the role of TPE/TPA in tuning their optical properties. Both TPE-MQ and TPA-MQ showed strong intramolecular charge transfer (ICT), which was confirmed through HOMO/LUMO calculations and solvent polarity dependent absorption studies. Interestingly, the acidic methylene of TPE-MQ was found to be highly sensitive to water compared to TPA-MQ. Even trace amounts of water in organic solvents acted as nucleophiles, leading to keto-enol formation and intense color due to enhanced π-conjugation. In contrast, TPA-MQ did not display any color or absorption changes in the presence of water. The water-induced structural conversion from keto to enol form was confirmed using 1H NMR analysis. Further, TPE-MQ colorimetric probe displayed clear color changes in presence of trace amounts of water in polar protic, aprotic, and non-polar solvents. Thus, the present study developed a simple naked-dye detecting colorimetric probe for sensing trace amount of water in organic solvents including commercial solvents.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors do not have permission to share data.

ce:section.ce:para: Water is a colorless, odorless, and tasteless liquid that exists in all three states on Earth. It's known as the universal solvent because of its ability to dissolve various substances due to hydrogen bonding and its benign nature towards living organisms. Over 70 % of the Earth's surface is covered with water. However, water is considered one of the most common contaminants of organic solvents (hydrophobic and hydrophilic) and dry products [1]. Water contamination in organic solvents also affects the chemical processes of various industries, such as laboratory chemistry, fine chemical industry, and liquid organic products [2–5]. Water contamination can significantly influence the chemical reactivity, quality, yield, and reliability of industrial products. The presence of even trace amounts of water in the reaction medium can pose significant safety risks, especially in sectors such as pharmaceutical manufacturing, chemical synthesis, and the food industry [6–9]. Consequently, there is an imperative to develop sensors that enable selective, rapid, and efficient detection of water content in organic solvents [10]. Karl Fisher titration is the primary method used for the quantitative analysis of water content in organic solvents [11–14]. This method exhibits reduced sensitivity to aprotic and non-alcoholic solvents. Additionally, various instrumental methods, such as gas chromatography (GC), nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR), UV–visible absorption spectroscopy, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR), and electrochemical techniques are utilized to detect and quantify water content in organic solvents [15–19]. Furthermore, colorimetric and fluorometric sensors have garnered notable interest due to their inherent advantages, including simplicity, high sensitivity and selectivity, easy sampling procedures, rapid response times, and visible color changes detectable by the naked eye, all while employing a non-destructive methodology [20–22]. An example of this is a pyrene–N-phenylparaphenylene diamine-derived imine conjugate fluorescence probe, which has shown effectiveness in quantitatively detecting water content in organic solvents and honey samples [23]. Research efforts have also investigated the application of organic fluorescent dyes and conjugated polymers for the detection of water in organic solvents, underscoring their notable sensitivity [24–27]. Furthermore, aggregation-induced emissive fluorophores have exhibited sensing trace amounts of water in organic solvents [28]. A fluorescence sensor that incorporates dual characteristics, leveraging both photo-induced electron transfer (PET) and aggregation-induced emission enhancement (AIEE), has been specifically developed for determining water content in a variety of organic solvents [29]. Moreover, several other fluorescence sensors employing PET mechanisms have been designed to proficiently detect water content in organic solvent [30,31]. Notably, molecules that exhibit excited state intramolecular proton transfer (ESIPT) show distinct variations in fluorescence sensitivity to water, attributed to the influence of solvent polarity on intramolecular hydrogen bonding interactions [32,33]. Carbon quantum dots exhibit notable biocompatibility, alongside robust fluorescence and high molar extinction coefficients, making them valuable for water sensing applications in organic solvents [34–37]. Similarly, fluorescent silver nanoclusters (Ag NCs) have been utilized for the colorimetric detection of water content in various solvent systems, including ethanol, dimethyl sulfoxide, white wine, and medical alcohol, with the aid of smartphone technology [38]. The preponderance of optical sensors developed for the detection of water in organic solvents predominantly employs fluorescence mechanisms, however, the literature on colorimetric sensors for this application remains limited [39,40]. The simplicity and convenience of colorimetric sensors have attracted considerable attention, establishing them as favourable alternatives to instrumental methods for the detection of water content in organic solvents [41]. In other words, a colorimetric sensor identifies color changes induced by the presence of water or moisture in organic solvents, which are perceptible to the naked eye, thereby avoiding the need for advanced instrumentation. In this context, it is noteworthy that metal-organic frameworks reveal pronounced color changes upon the incorporation of lattice water molecules in the solid state [42–44]. D-π-A type organic molecules are recognized for their strong intramolecular charge transfer (ICT) properties and exhibit distinct solvatochromism that is significantly influenced by the polarity of the solvent [45,46]. The optical properties of D-π-A sensors are subject to considerable alteration by variations in solvent environmental factors, including hydrogen bonding, polarity, viscosity, and pH. For example, zwitterionic pyridinium N-phenolate betaine dyes, which consist of an electron-donating phenolate group linked to an electron-accepting pyridinium ring, have displayed noticeable solvatochromism characterized by considerable color differentiation [47]. Various pyridinium-N-phenolate dyes, known as Reichardt betaine dyes, have been thoroughly studied for their unique solvatochromic properties [48]. In addition, N-phenacetyl viologen derivatives exhibit solvatochromism that is sensitive to changes in solvent polarity [49,50]. Additionally, N-phenacetyl viologen compounds containing highly reactive methylene groups can respond to external stimuli such as acid and base conditions, as well as temperature, resulting in observable color changes to the naked eye [51]. It has been found that the nucleophilicity of water can be significantly enhanced in the presence of organic solvents or within nano-channels, compared to bulk water [52,53]. It is anticipated that, this increased nucleophilicity allows water to react with the active methylene groups of N-phenacetyl viologen, leading to the formation of π-conjugated enolate compounds that display a dark color. The generation of these π-conjugated enolate is directly related to the water content in organic solvents, providing a strategic approach to developing a colorimetric sensor for detecting water content in organic solvents. Therefore, the development of robust donor-acceptor based N-phenacetyl viologens is expected to yield a sensing response to the presence of water in organic solvents. Therefore, in this manuscript, we focuses on the synthesis of two simple mono-quaternized 4-(4-pyridyl)pyridinium derivatives, 1-(2-oxo-2-(4-(1,2,2-triphenylvinyl)phenyl)ethyl)-[4,4′-bipyridin]-1-ium bromide (TPE-MQ) and 1-(2-(4-(diphenylamino)phenyl)-2-oxoethyl)-[4,4′-bipyridin]-1-ium bromide (TPA-MQ).

These compounds incorporate a mono-quaternized 4-(4-pyridyl)pyridinium electron acceptor, paired with either tetraphenylethylene (TPE) or triphenylamine (TPA) as electron donors, as depicted in Scheme 1 . Furthermore, this study investigates the donor-dependent solvatochromism and the water sensing capabilities of these compounds in organic solvents, with a focus on optimizing molecular design. Both TPE-MQ and TPA-MQ demonstrate significant alterations in absorption and color that are strongly influenced by the polarity of the solvent. The presence of trace amounts of water in organic solvents enhances the nucleophilicity of water, resulting in distinct color changes due to keto-enol formation in TPE-MQ solutions containing water. This characteristic color change renders TPE-MQ a promising candidate for selective water sensing in diverse organic solvents, regardless of polarity. In contrast, TPA-MQ remains stable, exhibiting no observable color change. This study aspires to provide foundational insights into the molecular design of advanced colorimetric probes for the detection of water in organic solvents.

All solvents and chemicals were purchased from commercial suppliers (Aldrich, TCI, Merck, Avra and HiMedia) and used as received. Fourier transformed infrared (FT-IR) measurements were performed using Shimadzu IR Affinity-1S spectrophotometer with KBr pellets. NMR spectra were recorded on Bruker Avance 400 MHz FT-NMR spectrometer. Mass spectra were recorded on an LC-MSD Trap XCT Plus based on infusion methods. The UV–Vis absorption spectra were recorded with UV–Vis–NIR spectrophotometer (Jasco V-770). Density functional theoretical (DFT) calculations were performed using the B3PW91/6-31+G(d,p) level of theory (Gaussian-09 (G09)).

Tetraphenylethylene (1.0 g, 3 mmol) was dissolved in dry CH2Cl2 (20 mL). To this solution, AlCl3 (0.41 g, 3 mmol) and bromoacetyl bromide (0.61 g, 3 mmol) was added and the reaction mixture was stirred at room temperature for 6h. The reaction progress was monitored by TLC. Upon completion of reaction, the reaction mixture was poured into ice water containing conc. HCl (5 mL). The produced was extracted with CH2Cl2. The organic phase was dried over anhydrous sodium sulphate (Na2SO4) and solvent was removed using rota-evaporator. The resulting crude product was purified using column chromatography using ethyl acetate: hexane (1:99) mixture eluent. The pure product was obtained as yellow crystalline solid. Yield: (0.6 g, 43 %). 1H NMR (400 MHz, CDCl3) δ: 7.72 (d, 2H, J = 8 Hz), 7.15 (m, 11H), 7.02 (d, 6H, J = 4 Hz), 4.38 (s, 2H). 13C NMR (100 MHz, CDCl3) δ: 190.1, 150.0, 143.1, 143.0, 142.0, 142.9, 139.6, 131.8, 131.3, 128.4, 127.9, 127.7, 127.1, 126.9, 126.9, 31.4. Anal. Calcd. for C28H21BrO: C, 74.18; H, 4.67 %. Found: C, 74.16; H, 4.68 %. LC-MS for C28H21BrO Calcd, [M+H]+ 452.08; Found, [M+H]+ 451.40. FT-IR (KBr, cm−1): ʋ = 3015(νC-H arom); 2954(νC-H aliph); 1685(νC
O); 1539, 1419 (νC
C).

Triphenylamine (1.0 g, 3 mmol) was dissolved in dry CH2Cl2 (20 mL). To this solution, AlCl3 (0.54 g, 4.0 mmol) and bromoacetyl bromide (0.81 g, 4.0 mmol) was added and the reaction mixture was stirred at room temperature for 1h. Then the mixture was continuously stirred at 45 °C for 24h. The reaction progress was monitored by TLC. Upon completion of reaction, the reaction mixture was poured into ice water containing conc. HCl (5 mL). The produced was extracted with CH2Cl2. The organic phase was dried over anhydrous sodium sulphate (Na2SO4) and solvent was removed using rota-evaporator. The resulting crude product was purified using column chromatography using ethyl acetate: hexane (2:98) mixture eluent. The pure product was obtained as yellow solid. Yield (0.55 g, 37 %). 1H NMR (400 MHz, CDCl3) δ: 7.75 (d, 2H, J = 9 Hz), 7.29 (t, 4H, J = 9 Hz), 7.18 (s, 1H), 7.11 (t, 3H, J = 9 Hz), 6.90 (d, 4H J = 9 Hz), 4.28 (s, 2H). 13C NMR (100 MHz, CDCl3) δ: 189.5, 152.8, 146.0, 130.6, 129.7, 126.4, 125.9, 125.1, 119.0, 30.6. Anal. Calcd. for C20H16BrNO: C, 65.59; H, 4.40; N, 3.82 %. Found: C, 65.55; H, 4.41; N, 3.81 %. LC-MS for C20H16BrNO Calcd, [M+H]+ 365.04; Found, [M+H]+ 366.85. FT-IR (KBr, cm−1): ʋ = 3128 (νC-H); 1600(νC
O); 1583, 1400 (νC
C).

BA-TPE (0.27 g, 0.6 mmol) was dissolved in dry acetone (12 mL) in a 25 mL round-bottom flask. Subsequently, 4,4′-bipyridine (0.048 g, 0.3 mmol) was added to the reaction mixture, which was then stirred at room temperature for a duration of 3 h under a nitrogen atmosphere. After completing the reaction, a pale-yellow precipitate was formed, which was then filtered and allowed to dry in the air. Yield (0.174 g, 92 %). 1H NMR (400 MHz, DMSO‑d 6) δ: 9.25 (d, 2H, J = 6 Hz), 8.90 (d, 2H, J = 6 Hz), 7.91 (d, 2H, J = 9 Hz), 7.30 (d, 2H, J = 9 Hz), 7.18 (t, 12H, J = 9 Hz), 7.08 (d, 2H, J = 3 Hz), 7.06 (d, 2H, J = 3 Hz), 7.01 (t, 3H, J = 9 Hz), 6.48 (s, 2H). 13C NMR (100 MHz, DMSO‑d 6) δ: 190.1, 150.5, 149.9, 147.7, 143.1, 142.9, 139.9, 131.9, 131.8, 131.1, 131.0, 128.6, 128.4, 127.6, 127.5, 127.0, 66.8. Anal. Calcd. for C38H29BrN2O: C, 74.88; H, 4.80; N, 4.65 %. Found: C, 74.92; H, 4.79; N, 4.64 %. ESI-MS for C38H29N2O Calcd, [M‒Br]+ 529.2275; Found, [M‒Br]+ 529.2329. FT-IR (KBr, cm−1): ʋ = 3437(νC-H); 1691(νC
O); 1635(νC
N); 1599(νC
N +); 1556, 1490, 1446 (νC
C).

BA-TPA (0.140 g, 0.3 mmol) was dissolved in dry acetone (12 mL) in a 25 mL round-bottom flask. Subsequently, 4,4′-bipyridine (0.03 g, 0.15 mmol) was added to the reaction mixture, which was then stirred at room temperature for a duration of 3 h under a nitrogen atmosphere. After completing the reaction, a olive green colored precipitate was formed, which was then filtered and allowed to dry in the air. Yield (0.087 g, 86 %). 1H NMR (400 MHz, DMSO‑d 6) δ: 9.13 (d, 2H, J = 6 Hz), 8.91 (d, 2H, J = 6 Hz), 8.75 (d, 2H J = 6 Hz), 8.09 (d, 2H, J = 6 Hz), 7.95 (d, 2H, J = 9 Hz), 7.46 (t, 4H, J = 6 Hz), 7.23 (m, 6H), 6.97 (d, 2H, J = 6 Hz), 6.39 (s, 2H). 13C NMR (100 MHz, DMSO‑d 6) δ: 188.4, 153.3, 151.5, 147.2, 145.9, 130.7, 130.6, 126.8, 126.0, 125.5, 122.4, 118.5, 65.8. Anal. Calcd. for C30H24BrN3O: C, 68.97; H, 4.63; N, 8.04 %. Found: C, 69.02; H, 4.64; N, 8.06 %. LC-MS for [C30H24N3O]+ Calcd, [M‒Br]+ 442.1914; Found, [M‒Br]+ 442.1972. FT-IR (KBr, cm−1): ʋ = 3451(νC-H); 1674 (νC
O); 1639 (νC
N); 1587, 1494 (νC
C).

2-Bromoacetophenone functionalized tetraphenylethylene (TPE) and triphenylamine (TPA) derivatives, referred to as BA-TPE and BA-TPA were synthesized using a previously reported procedure from our research group [54]. The mono-quaternized N-phenacyl-4-(4-pyridyl)pyridinium bromide derivatives, TPE-MQ and TPA-MQ, were synthesized by reacting 4,4′-bipyridine with either BA-TPE or BA-TPA in anhydrous acetonitrile at 90 °C for 3 h. This reaction was also performed in anhydrous acetone at room temperature, as shown in Scheme 2 . When acetonitrile was used as the solvent, the reaction primarily produced a mono-quaternary N-phenacyl-4-(4-pyridyl)pyridinium salt as the major product. Additionally, a minor product, the bi-quaternary 4,4′-bipyridinium salt, was also formed. However, both products are insoluble in anhydrous acetonitrile, making their separation challenging. In contrast, when the reaction was conducted in anhydrous acetone at room temperature, it selectively yielded the mono-quaternary N-phenacyl-4-(4-pyridyl)pyridinium salt without producing the bi-quaternary 4,4′-bipyridinium salt, which also remains insoluble in anhydrous acetone [55]. The synthesized compounds, TPE-MQ and TPA-MQ, were characterized using FT-IR, 1H and 13C NMR, and mass spectral analysis. The FT-IR analysis of TPE-MQ and TPA-MQ shows that the absorption bands at 1691 cm−1 and 1674 cm−1 correspond to the fundamental vibrations of the ν(CO) functional group. The lower vibrational frequency indicates the presence of N-phenacyl pyridinium ketones, as well as a carbonyl functional group that is conjugated with a benzene ring. Both compounds exhibit two distinct vibrational frequency bands. The first band, observed in the range of 1635–1639 cm−1, corresponds to the free CN stretch (ν(CN)). The second band occurs in the range of 1587–1599 cm−1 and is associated with the N-alkylated CN+ stretch (ν(CN+)). Notably, the N-alkylated ν(CN+) group demonstrates a lower vibrational frequency than the free ν(CN) group. This observation suggests a decreased double bond character for the ν(CN+) group. Therefore, the FT-IR results confirm the presence of both CN and CN+ groups, thereby supporting the formation of monoquaternized 4-(4-pyridyl)pyridinium units (Fig. S2 and S4). 1H NMR analysis showed a singlet signal at 6.48 ppm for TPE-MQ and 6.39 ppm for TPA-MQ, which correspond to the CH2 protons of the 2-oxoethyl (–CH2–CO) group. The chemical shifts of aromatic protons adjacent to the pyridinium nitrogen were observed in the range between 9.25 and 9.23 ppm for TPE-MQ and 9.11 and 9.13 ppm for TPA-MQ. The downfield chemical shift of the pyridinium protons suggests that one of the nitrogens in the 4,4′-bipyridine unit has become electron deficient due to N-alkylation. TPE-MQ and TPA-MQ exhibited three doublet signals between δ 8.90–7.27 ppm and 8.91–8.07 ppm, which are attributed to the remaining aromatic protons of the 4,4′-bipyridine unit. The upfield chemical shift indicates that one pyridine moiety in the 4,4′-bipyridine unit remains free from N-alkylation. Peaks for the aromatic protons of TPE and TPA were found in the range of 7.95–6.95 ppm. The representative 1H NMR spectra of TPE-MQ and TPA-MQ are shown in Fig. S5 and S7. In the 13C NMR spectra of TPE-MQ and TPA-MQ showed aromatic carbon peaks between 153.33 and 118.51 ppm and –CH2- carbon of 2-oxoethyl group at 66.79 and 65.79 ppm (Fig. S6 and S8).

The ESI-MS analysis revealed peaks at 529.25 and 442.19 corresponding to the organic cations [C38H29N2O]+ and [C30H24N3O]+ of TPE-MQ and TPA-MQ, respectively (Fig. S9 and S10). Thus, the mass spectra further confirmed the formation of mono-quaternized 4-(4-pyridyl)pyridinium derivatives. In both TPE-MQ and TPA-MQ, the TPE or TPA moieties are connected to the mono-quaternized 4-(4-pyridyl)pyridinium via a 2-oxoethyl (–CH2–CO) bridge, which possesses acidic methylene hydrogens. The quaternization of the nitrogen atom within the 4-(4-pyridyl)pyridinium structure is expected to further enhance the electron-withdrawing characteristics, thereby facilitating improved intramolecular charge transfer. The methylene protons of the 2-oxoethyl group might undergo deprotonation in the presence of nucleophiles, including mild bases. This could lead to the formation of an extended π-conjugated push-pull type enol compound (TPE-MQ-E) and can produce visible color change (Scheme 3 ) [56].

TPE-MQ and TPA-MQ, incorporated triphenylamine (TPA)/tetraphenylethylene (TPE) donor units with mono-quaternized 4-(4-pyridyl) pyridinium acceptor units via a 2-oxoethyl (–CH2–CO) moiety, is expected to display intramolecular charge transfer (ICT). Absorption studies were performed for TPE-MQ and TPA-MQ in various solvent environments to further assess the robustness of the ICT phenomenon. Notably, TPE-MQ exhibited solvent polarity absorption (1.0 × 10−5 M) and color changes (Fig. 1 and Table S1).

TPE-MQ displayed a violet color in DMF, while a pale violet color was observed in DMSO. TPE-MQ exhibited a π–π∗ transition at shorter wavelengths, between 335 and 350 nm, and a red-shifted n-π∗ transition that varied with solvent polarity, occurring at wavelengths from 475 to 603 nm (Fig. 1 and Table S1). TPE-MQ exhibited a significant red-shift in the intramolecular charge transfer (ICT) absorption band, appearing at 570 nm, 575 nm, and 582 nm in ethanol, CH3CN, and acetone, respectively. In contrast, the TPE-MQ solution in DMF and DMSO showed two ICT peaks, a strong peak at 509 nm and a weak broad peak around 600 nm. On the other hand, TPA-MQ did not demonstrate any noticeable color change across the different solvents. It appeared strong yellow in CH2Cl2 and CHCl3, while it appeared pale pink in DMF (Fig. S13a). The solvent polarity dependent absorption spectra indicated only a small shift in absorption for TPA-MQ compared to TPE-MQ (Fig. S13b). Notably, the active methylene protons (2-oxoethyl) adjacent to the 4-(4-pyridyl)pyridinium moiety exhibit more acidic in nature, enabling deprotonation depending upon the nucleophilicity or polarity of the solvent employed [56,57]. The deprotonation process can lead to the formation of enol structure with extended π-conjugation. This structural transformation can result in the red shift of absorption and appearance of intense coloration. Interestingly, the presence of trace amounts of water in organic solvents caused significant color changes with TPE-MQ, compared to the results obtained with dry solvents, as shown in Fig. S12. In DMF, the TPE-MQ solution changed from weak violet to dark brown upon the introduction of water. Similarly, the TPE-MQ in DMSO shifted from pale violet to a deep dark gray color when water was added. Furthermore, adding a trace amount of water to a nearly colorless mixture of CH3CN and EtOH resulted in transparent violet and yellow colors. When water was added into DCM and CHCl3, both are immiscible with water, it showed intense violet color. In contrast, adding water to TPA-MQ did not lead to a significant color change (Fig. S14). This observation indicates that the active methylene protons of TPE-MQ may be more acidic than those in TPA-MQ. The incorporation of TPE enhanced acidity, allowing it to react with water as a nucleophile. This leads to the formation of an enolate through the keto-enol conversion, resulting in the intense color observed. To confirm the water-induced formation of the enolate in TPE-MQ in organic solvents, 1H NMR analysis was performed (Fig. 2 ).

The 1H NMR spectrum of TPE-MQ in DMSO‑d 6 was recorded both with and without the addition of external water (Fig. 2). In the absence of water, the methylene protons in TPE-MQ resonated as a singlet at δ 6.48 ppm. However, upon adding water, new peaks were observed at δ 6.33 and 6.25 ppm. Additionally, the enolic OH proton appeared at δ 9.08 ppm. The peaks intensity at δ 6.33 and 6.25 ppm, corresponding to the keto and enol methylene was used to calculate the keto and enol ratio in the solution. It showed a ratio of approximately 62 % keto and 38 % enol form. It is noted that keto form is relatively more stable compared to enol form.

The previously reported viologen systems also exhibited intense coloration due to the formation of keto-enol structures; however, this effect was only observed in basic solvents or environments [51,56]. There have been no studies reported on color changes of organic molecules induced by water. The distinct color change of TPE-MQ upon the addition of trace amounts of water in organic solvents was further employed for water sensing in organic solvents. In spectroscopic-grade ethanol, TPE-MQ showed relatively weak absorption at 567 nm, resulting in a very light color for the solution (Fig. 3 a). When 1.49 wt % of water was added, there was a slight decrease of absorption at 567 nm, accompanied by the emergence of a new peak at 488 nm. As the concentration of water increased, the longer wavelength absorption band gradually decreased, while the absorbance intensity at 488 nm increased. The absorption peak at the longer wavelength completely disappeared after adding 1.49 wt % of water, and a strong peak at 488 nm emerged, resulting in a clear yellow solution. The strong increase of π-π∗ transition, which is an allowed transition, contributed for the appearance of strong color in the solution.

The limit of detection (LOD) demonstrated that TPE-MQ can effectively sense water concentrations as low as 0.0070 wt % in ethanol (Fig. 3b). In CH3CN, TPE-MQ exhibited strong intramolecular charge transfer (ICT) absorption at 575 nm, and the solution appeared nearly colorless (Fig. 4 ). The addition of water led to a slight reduction in ICT peak intensity, accompanied by increased absorption between 300 and 360 nm. When 11.68 wt % of water was added, TPE-MQ displayed a significantly reduced intensity at longer wavelengths, while absorption at shorter wavelength peaks increased.

The analysis demonstrated enhanced absorption between 400 and 600 nm, resulting in a violet-colored solution. The limit of detection (LOD) analysis revealed that water can be detected at levels as low as 0.072 wt % in CH3CN (Fig. S15). Similarly, the TPE-MQ in DMF exhibited a strong peak at 507 nm, along with a broad red-shifted peak between 590 and 630 nm and appeared light violet color (Fig. 5 a). The intensity of the red-shifted broad peak gradually decreased upon the addition of water, while the peak at 507 nm remained relatively unchanged. The addition of 0.16 wt % of water led to a significant reduction in the peak intensity at longer wavelengths. The longer wavelength absorption was completely disappeared by the addition of 1.66 wt % of water, resulting in a dark brown solution. The LOD analysis indicated that TPE-MQ probe can detect water up to 0.0090 wt % in DMF (Fig. S16). The DMSO-TPE-MQ solution displayed a very light violet color (Fig. 5b). Its absorption spectrum showed a strong peak at 507 nm, with a considerably weaker and broader peak at longer wavelengths compared to the DMF solution (Fig. 5b). Unlike DMF, the addition of water to DMSO increased the intensity at 507 nm and produced a clear peak at longer wavelengths of 633 nm, resulting in a brownish-violet color due to dual absorption at 507 nm and 633 nm. The TPE-MQ probe was capable of detecting up to 0.0026 wt % of water in DMSO (Fig. S17). TPE-MQ also exhibited distinct color changes in CHCl3 and CH2Cl2 while adding water, though they were immiscible (Fig. S18a and S18b). In CHCl3, TPE-MQ showed a weak intramolecular charge transfer (ICT) transition at 603 nm, which was intensified upon adding water and the initial colorless solution transformed into an intense violet. In CH2Cl2, TPE-MQ displayed a strong π-π∗ transition and a very weak ICT band around 580 nm (Fig. S18b). When water was added, there was an increase in absorption at longer wavelengths, leading to the formation of an intense violet color solution.

To gain insight in the intramolecular charge transfer (ICT) from the TPA/TPE donor to the mono-quaternized 4,4′-bipyridine acceptor, density functional theoretical (DFT) calculations was performed for TPE-MQ and TPA-MQ using their optimized structures (Fig. 6 a and b). The highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) of both molecules indicated that the electron density was primarily concentrated in the donor units (TPE/TPA, Fig. 6a and b). In contrast, the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) analysis for TPE-MQ and TPA-MQ demonstrated that the electron density was completely transferred to the mono-quaternized 4,4′-bipyridine unit. Notably, TPE-MQ exhibits a lower optical band gap of 1.06 eV, while TPA-MQ has a band gap of 1.43 eV. Analysis of the HOMO-LUMO orbitals confirmed strong intramolecular charge transfer (ICT) in both molecules. However, TPE-MQ exhibited a lower HOMO-LUMO energy gap, attributed to a significant enhancement of π-conjugation between the TPE unit and the pyridinium moiety.

Further to understand the enol tautomerism induced color change, DFT calculations were also conducted for optimized structures of TPE-MQ and TPA-MQ enol derivatives (TPE-MQ-E and TPA-MQ-E). Similar to keto derivatives, the electron density was mainly occupied at the TPA/TPE donor in HOMO and shifted to pyridinium part in the LUMO (Fig. 6c and d). However, the enol derivatives showed significantly low optical band gap compared to keto form and supported the intense color appearance. TPE-MQ-E and TPA-MQ-E showed optical band gap of 0.53 and 0.61 eV, respectively. Thus, the TPE-MQ probe sensitively respond to water contamination in organic solvents and produced enol derivative with visually detectable intense color. However, the limitation of the present probe is lack of fluorescence in solution as well as solid-state, which could be a more sensitive method than colorimetric.

In summary, we have synthesized two monoquarternized 4-(4-pyridyl)pyridinium derivatives (referred to as probes), namely TPE-MQ and TPA-MQ. These derivatives exhibit solvatochromism that varies depending on the nature of their substituents. Notably, the incorporation of the TPE unit into the 4-(4-pyridyl)pyridinium framework enhanced the acidity of the methylene protons. This enhancement facilitates their interaction with water, leading to the formation of an enol structure characterized by intense coloration in organic solvents. This significant color change has been effectively utilized for the visual detection of trace amounts of water in organic solvents. The TPE-MQ probe demonstrated notable color variations in response to water in various organic solvents. For example, colorless ethanol turned yellow, while CH3CN changed to violet. Additionally, TPE-MQ exhibited a dark brown color in DMF and a deep violet shade in DMSO. Water-dependent absorption studies revealed significant changes with the addition of merely a few microliters of water, with a limit of detection (LOD) indicating that TPE-MQ can successfully sense down to 0.0090 wt % in DMF. NMR analysis corroborated the conversion from the keto to the enol structure in the presence of water. Consequently, the monoquarternized 4-(4-pyridyl)pyridinium compound, TPE-MQ, demonstrates promising characteristics as a solvatochromic organic material, which can be effectively utilized for the colorimetric detection of trace levels of water in various organic solvents.

Karolin P. Jose: Validation, Methodology, Formal analysis. R. Rameshbabu Priyadharsan: Validation, Formal analysis. Kamalanathan Chidambaranathan: Validation, Investigation. Subramanian Karthikeyan: Validation, Methodology, Data curation. Savarimuthu Philip Anthony: Writing – review & editing, Software, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation. Vedichi Madhu: Writing – original draft, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

VM also acknowledges the UGC-DAE Consortium for Scientific Research in Indore for their financial support (CRS/2021-22/04/641). Additionally, KPJ thanks the Karunya Institute of Technology and Sciences for providing research associate support.
